data.title: World of Travel: Inside Denver, the Capital of the New West
data.description:
Denver has been quietly redefining itself.

Once known as the “Queen City of the Plains,” Denver is now one of the fastest growing metro areas in the country, a place where migration, innovation, and cultural reckoning are colliding in real time.

As people continue moving west in search of opportunity, space, and quality of life, Denver finds itself at the center of a national conversation about growth. Who gets to belong. How history is honored. What progress actually looks like.

It is a familiar position for a city built on reinvention.

From frontier outpost to railroad hub, from mining capital to cultural crossroads, Denver has never stayed still for long. What feels different now is the scale. The city is no longer just a gateway to the Rockies, it is a destination shaping the future of the American West while grappling honestly with its past. That tension between legacy and possibility is what makes Denver feel so current.

With partners like Visit Denver helping spotlight its cultural evolution and platforms like GetYourGuide making its layered history more accessible to visitors, Denver is inviting the world to see it with fresh eyes.

Where Time Begins: Dinosaurs at the Edge of the City

Just outside the city, at Dinosaur Ridge, 150 million years of Earth’s story are etched into stone.

What appears to be a quiet hillside is one of North America’s most significant paleontological sites. The dinosaur footprints were discovered in the 1930s when crews were building the road to Red Rocks.

“In the 1930s when they were making this road to Red Rocks they were blasting it with dynamite and the workers actually uncovered the footprints,” says Jeremy Jenkin, owner of Amazing Adventures Colorado, who guides visitors through the site’s layered history.

But the drama does not stop there.

“In the 1800s, that’s when the field of paleontology was growing,” Jenkin explains. “Two guys had come out here and they had such a bitter rivalry that when they would excavate an area, they would dynamite it when they’d leave so the other guy wouldn’t even find it.”

At Dinosaur Ridge, you see more than bones. You see ego. Ambition. Competition. Discovery. The story of science unfolding in real time.  And you begin to understand something essential about Colorado.

Reinvention is not new here.

Red Rocks: Sacred Ground, Global Stage

A few miles away rises one of the most iconic music venues in the world, Red Rocks Amphitheatre.

Today it is synonymous with legendary performances. But long before the concerts, this land was sacred.

So if you think about Red Rocks, and you think about places like Roxborough Park, all along the Front Range, there were sacred sites that were very important to us,” says Rick Williams, CEO of People of Sacred Land and a member of the Oglala Lakota Nation, who also has Cheyenne ancestry. “That’s where we would go to pray, and that’s where we would go to honor our ancestors.

“And today, those places are state parks or they’re concert venues. Even to go there to pray, we have to pay,” he said. “Those were our churches, and people don’t understand that. Those areas were sacred to us, and they always will be sacred to us. Each one of those rock formations takes on a special meaning to us. People who go there see it as, ‘Oh, this is nice,’ but they don’t see it as sacred. They don’t see the sanctity of it.”

That perspective changes the way you see the amphitheater. It is not just a bucket list concert venue. It is ancestral land that carries spiritual memory.  Opera singers once performed here in the early 1900s. The Beatles famously played for two dollars a ticket in 1964. The stage evolved. The meaning beneath it did not disappear.  Red Rocks is both celebration and contradiction. That duality defines Denver’s story.

The Complicated Legacy of the Old West

Denver also carries the name of one of the West’s most famous figures, Buffalo Bill Cody.

Cody was a global celebrity who exported the mythology of the American West to more than 1,400 cities worldwide. But mythology and reality do not always align.

“You know, I think it is a great example of how many Colorado historical figures are complicated,” says Denver Mayor Mike Johnston.

He points out that Colorado was the first state where the male electorate voted to grant women the right to vote. At the same time, he acknowledges the darker truths.

“Certainly Buffalo Bill was part of the raiding and depletion of the buffalo stock,” Johnston says. “There were certainly hard parts of that history, including violence against Native individuals and exploitation in some of those early shows.”

The mayor adds that the goal is not erasure, but accountability.

“We try to acknowledge all the hard truths about the Buffalo Bill story, and share the parts of the legacy to hold on to, and the parts that are there to learn from,” he says. “There was a time when Denver was the capital of the Old West. Now we want to position Denver as the capital of the New West. A place where everyone can find opportunity. A place that is inclusive. A place that sees everybody.”

That evolution is the thread connecting past and present.

Freedom, Experimentation, and the Marijuana Mile

Colorado’s rebellious streak did not end with the frontier.

In 2012, the state became the first in the nation to legalize recreational marijuana. What began as a social experiment became a regulated industry that reshaped Denver’s economy.

“When I started it was completely different,” says Jenna Green of Colorado Cannabis Tours. “It was just medical. There were no grow facilities. People were still growing in their closets and bringing in huge trash bags full of weed. There was no lab. There was no testing. I was the lab.”

The industry evolved quickly.

“Now you had to have a lot of money to build out a grow and start a dispensary,” Green explains. “There were limited licenses. So instead of spending $25,000 to the state, you’re spending $2.5 million to an individual.”

That shift brought corporate consolidation and new barriers to entry. But Green says the state’s personality remains intact.

“I like our slightly rebellious attitude,” she says. “Here in Colorado, we have a reputation for bending the rules. We were one of the first states to introduce and break prohibition. We had the first marijuana arrest and we were the first to legalize it. There’s this entrepreneurial, on the edge spirit. A rabble rouser energy.”

That energy is visible across the city, from former warehouses turned cultural hubs to tour experiences booked through platforms like GetYourGuide, connecting visitors to a once underground economy that is now mainstream.

The Capital of the New West

Denver today is deeply connected to land, elevation and open sky. It is also connected to the world through one of the busiest airports on the planet and a rapidly diversifying economy.

“I think at every turn when Denver’s faced challenges, it’s found a way to reinvent itself,” Mayor Johnston says. “It has entrepreneurs and civic leaders who are committed to something bigger than themselves. That’s what gives Denver that big city with a small town feeling.”

The New West, as he describes it, is inclusive. It welcomes everyone. It honors complexity instead of smoothing it over.

From dinosaur tracks uncovered by dynamite, to sacred lands transformed into stages, to frontier legends reconsidered, to modern industries born out of rebellion, Denver does not erase its layers.  It lives inside them.  And in doing so, it offers a vision of what reinvention can look like when a city is willing to face its past while building its future.

EDITOR’S NOTE: Lindsey Granger and Kolyn Boyd are the co-creators of “World of Travel,” a series that explores the past, present and future of extraordinary places around the globe. You can watch “The Heartbeat of Denver” and future episodes each Wednesday at 4 p.m. EDT on The Hill’s YouTube page.

Lindsey Granger is the co-host of “Rising.” Kolyn Boyd is a film director based in Washington, D.C.

Catch complimentary podcasts every Thursday at 4 p.m. on The Hill’s YouTube channel.

The Denver episode of “World of Travel” was sponsored by GetYourGuide, Visit Denver, Amazing Adventures Colorado, Colorado Cannabis Tours, Schutz Glass

data.title: Khanna, Massie plan to force war powers vote on Iran
data.description:
Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) and Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) plan to move next week to force a vote on a resolution to require authorization from Congress before President Trump can use military force against Iran.

“Trump officials say there’s a 90% chance of strikes on Iran. He can’t without Congress,” Khanna said in a post on X on Wednesday. “@RepThomasMassie & I have a War Powers Resolution to debate & vote on war before putting U.S. troops in harm’s way. I will make a motion to discharge to force a vote on it next week.”

In the closely-divided House, the vote could be very close. Republicans can afford no more than one GOP defection on any party-line vote, assuming all members are present and voting, and absences can change that margin.

The U.S. has built up warships and other military forces near Iran, even as it engages in talks about limiting Iran’s nuclear program. Trump last week said that regime change in Iran “would be the best thing that could happen.”

Khanna pointed to his resolution with Massie, H.Con.Res. 38, that was introduced in June 2025 during the Twelve-Day War between Israel and Iran, before the U.S. struck Iran’s nuclear sites. It has 76 other Democratic cosponsors, and no other Republican cosponsors.

The resolution directs the president to “terminate the use of United States Armed Forces from hostilities against the Islamic Republic of Iran or any part of its government or military, unless explicitly authorized by a declaration of war or specific authorization for use of military force against Iran.” It clarifies that the resolution would not prevent the U.S. from defending itself, or disrupt U.S. intelligence gathering and sharing.

“Congress must vote on war according to our Constitution. @RepRoKhanna and I will be forcing that vote to happen in the House as soon as possible,” Massie added on X. “I will vote to put America first which means voting against more war in the Middle East.”

War powers resolutions are privileged parliamentary procedures in the House and Senate, allowing members to bypass House Republican leaders to force floor votes on the matter.

But while Democrats have moved to force several such votes, they have not been successful.

Last month, the House narrowly voted down a resolution designed to limit Trump’s ability to use military power in Venezuela. Republicans successfully blocked the measure in a 215-215 tie vote. Two Republicans — Massie and Rep. Don Bacon (Neb.) — joined all 213 Democrats in voting for the measure.

“Like the votes before the Iraq war, this could be one of the most consequential votes in the history of Congress. Are we going to stop another endless dumb foreign war? Or will the neoconservatives mislead us once again?” Khanna said.

data.title: Groundhog Day: How House members and senators exploit procedure to get their way
data.description:
Last week, the House of Representatives was rocking with procedural jockeying while the Senate sat spinning its wheels over how to keep the Department of Homeland Security running after its funding expired at midnight Friday. It did not succeed and the department technically shuttered its doors while negotiations continue to further restrict immigration agents in the wake of the Minneapolis riots and killings of two U.S. citizens.

The House saga began on Monday when the Rules Committee reported a special rule to allow the consideration of three bills on firearms, undersea fiber optic cables, and critical energy resources. The rule also self-executed the adoption of language to extend from Feb. 10 through July, a freeze on any House attempts to call-up as privileged any resolution to terminate the president’s use of emergency economic powers to set global tariffs using executive orders.

House leadership had imposed the original freeze early last year, to protect Trump’s unilateral tariff-setting orders, beginning with Canada on Feb. 1. The language was befitting of Groundhog Day, freezing the same legislative day in place and thus effectively preventing members from calling up tariff termination resolutions on the floor.

Last September, the House extended the freeze from the end of the year through Feb. 10 of this year. When that special rule came to the House floor on Feb. 10 for a further extension through July, it was surprisingly defeated 214-217, with three Republicans joining all Democrats to defeat it.

That in turn thawed the deep freeze, opening the door for considering termination of the president’s first executive order imposing tariffs on Canada. The next day, the termination resolution was called-up by unanimous consent and went on to pass, 219-211, this time with six Republicans breaking party ranks.

The Senate had already passed an identical resolution on Oct. 30, and two other termination resolutions earlier that week. The tariff tremors on the Hill ran across both houses. These are probably pyrrhic victories, since the termination measures will not be enacted over Trump’s likely veto. What is more likely is that the Supreme Court will pull the plug on the president by upholding a U.S. international trade court’s finding that his “Liberation Day” global tariffs are unlawful. The high court heard oral arguments on Nov. 5 at the president’s request for an emergency expedited hearing, during which three of the conservative justices expressed skepticism at the president’s justifications.

Last Wednesday, the House took another procedural leap by substituting the text of the SAVE America Act (and acronym for Save American Voter Eligibility) for a Senate-passed bill to establish a veterans’ advisory committee on equal access. The bill requires proof of citizenship to register to vote and a photo ID to actually vote. This shell game was probably designed to help expedite passage when the bill returns to the Senate.

A few Republicans had tried to bring down the rule for the last tranche of appropriations bills on Feb. 4 because it did not include the SAVE Act language. Only with a leadership pledge to promptly provide a separate SAVE Act vote last week did three of the four GOP holdouts switch their votes and pass the rule, 217-214.

The Senate was unable last Thursday to muster a three-fifths vote to invoke cloture (shut down a filibuster) on the Homeland Security funding bill. But ironically, generous funding for tougher anti-immigration measures had already been included in last year’s omnibus tax relief bill, including $75 billion for ICE. So even though ICE is funded, Homeland Security personnel at the Transportation Security Administration, FEMA and the Coast Guard have been working without pay since the shutdown began. Both houses adjourned last Thursday for a week’s break, leaving those unpaid workers in the lurch.

The contrasts between House and Senate procedures are usually stark. The unified pushback against Trump’s tariff actions these last three months is a rare exception. The House ordinarily moves swiftly, needing only majority votes to impose even bizarre procedural fixes. The Senate moves more ponderously under its supermajority voting requirements. They are still two distinct bodies inhabiting separate chambers together.

Don Wolfensberger is a 28-year congressional staff veteran culminating as chief of staff of the House Rules Committee in 1995. He is author of, “Congress and the People: Deliberative Democracy on Trial” (2000), and, “Changing Cultures in Congress: From Fair Play to Power Plays” (2018).

data.title: O'Reilly urges Stephen A. Smith to run for White House
data.description:
Political commentator Bill O’Reilly is urging Stephen A. Smith to seriously consider a bid for the White House in 2028.

“I think he should run, and I’m being serious now,” O’Reilly told NewsNation host Chris Cuomo.

“[President Donald] Trump opened the door for you,” he quipped, speaking directly to Smith. “You should send Trump a big bouquet of flowers. He opened the door for you.”

Smith was discussing his political aspirations in an appearance on “Cuomo,” days after he once again hinted at a possible presidential run.

“Once upon a time, it was emphatically no. That has quelled to some degree,” the sports commentator said, though emphasizing that he was not eager to be “interrupting my quality of life.”

O’Reilly demurred at Smith’s electoral prospects but suggested that he may be an effective disruptor in national politics.

“You can run, and you, but don’t have any expectation of winning,” the former Fox News host said.

“See, you’re what you’re doing is you’re taking a furlough from your dopey sports show, and you’re saying, I’ll be back, because I’m not going to win,” O’Reilly continued. “But you’re injecting yourself into the debate, the national debate, and you’re exposing the charlatans, which would be an amazing historical achievement.”

Smith, known for his firebrand style, said earlier in the show that he would “give anything to be on a debate stage” with aspiring politicians, wishing them “good luck” if he decides to jump in.  

“If you ever put me on that debate stage with some of these politicians aspiring for high office, with the nonsense that they’ve engaged in, with the behavior they’ve exercised, with the tendencies that they’ve displayed, and the lack of consideration to the American people going up against me, good luck,” he added.

The ESPN commentator also cast himself as someone who “has America’s best interest at heart,” arguing that the open-mindedness that helped him build a television audience would be an asset on the national stage.

“We should be listening to people on both sides. Anybody that has America’s best interest at heart is who we should care to listen to. I would be that guy,” Smith continued. “That’s the reason why I leave the door open and entertain it, even though I sincerely doubt I will ever be willing to give up my money to run for office.”

Still, he told O’Reilly that if he launched a bid, he intended to finish on top.

“I don’t play to lose…and if I decide to do it, I’m telling you I intend to win,” he said. “Make no mistake about it. I won’t do it unless I believe I have a legitimate shot to win it, the presidency.”

data.title: Natural gas, renewables can help Democrats on energy affordability
data.description:
Across the country, Americans are facing a new energy reality: electric bills are rising and the risk of blackouts is growing as our power grid faces unprecedented demand. The recent devastating winter storm put that system to the test, as utilities struggled to keep the lights on and homes heated in the harshest conditions. Without pragmatic solutions, growing strain from rising demand — driven in part by artificial intelligence and data center growth — will keep increasing energy costs for families and businesses unless we invest in more firm power sources. 

Winter Storm Fern, which swept across two-thirds of the country, underscored the consequences of blocking natural gas infrastructure over the past decade. Blue states like New Jersey saw about 25 to 30 percent of their power being supplied by midwestern coal plants. In New England, some 40 percent of the power came from higher-emitting fuel oil because the region lacks access to natural gas, while renewable sources like solar and wind were largely unavailable.

Most of the renewable sources that did operate were wood-burning and trash incinerators. Even the much-touted Canada-to-New England transmission line transporting hydropower — celebrated as a step toward a more reliable, affordable and cleaner grid — shut down during the storm just over a week after it had begun commercial operation. Years of opposing natural gas in the name of climate policy thus made things worse across New England.

This moment presents an opportunity for Democratic leaders to reset the national energy conversation. Lowering costs, maintaining reliability, and advancing climate goals are not mutually exclusive, but doing all three calls for policy rooted in reality. An all-of-the-above energy approach that pairs renewable energy with dependable sources available 24/7 like natural gas is the most practical path forward to help decarbonize and cut costs, without sacrificing reliability.

We all want to see meaningful progress on climate. But states that set ambitious climate targets are already feeling the tension between aspiration and economic reality. In New York, a recent Progressive Policy Institute report concluded that aggressive decarbonization mandates are placing significant cost burdens on ratepayers. In New Jersey, another such analysis found that rising electricity prices amid capacity constraints and surging demand are challenging affordability as leaders pursue clean energy goals. These stateside examples represent a national affordability challenge where we as Democrats must strike the right balance.

Virginia, America’s data center capital, is no stranger to growing grid demand and has the opportunity to lead on pragmatic solutions. Democratic Gov. Abigail Spanberger’s (D) recognition of the continued role of natural gas puts her ahead of many in national energy policy debates. While about a third of Virginia households rely on natural gas for home heating, a majority of the commonwealth’s electricity is generated from it.

The Trump administration’s decision to pause Dominion Energy’s $11.2 billion offshore wind project, even briefly, sent a troubling signal about the risks of politicizing energy sources at a time when reliability and affordability matter most. Unfortunately, politicization has not been limited to one party. Extreme voices in the Democratic Party have also forced leaders into a box on energy policy by opposing natural gas and demanding a renewables-only future.

These policies were implemented over the last decade, even though natural gas has been the greatest driver of power sector carbon emission reductions, which are down 41 percent over the last 20 years. But these renewable-only positions are failing particularly across blue states in the northeast. As energy bills skyrocket, renewables have been slow to scale and meet demand challenges, and concerns are rising about grid reliability. Democratic governors are now opening the door once again to building more natural gas infrastructure.

Natural gas power plants can turn on quickly and scale up or down to match second by second fluctuations in grid demand during periods when renewable sources aren’t generating electricity. Used as a foundation, natural gas enables states to deploy more renewables without sacrificing grid reliability or affordability.

These reliability and affordability pressures are particularly acute in areas with heavy demand growth. Virginia already hosts more than 600 data centers — the densest concentration in the nation — which are consuming electricity at unprecedented rates and putting upward pressure on costs. This trend mirrors what communities in other states are experiencing as digital infrastructure expands faster than grid upgrades.

As demand surges, so will costs, and affordability has become a defining issue heading into the 2026 midterms. Families are seeing rising electricity bills attributed in part to the increasing energy demands of data centers and infrastructure upgrades needed to support them. Nearly one in twenty households in the U.S. have severe utility debt. Those with the highest energy cost burdens — primarily Black and brown households — are most at risk in an energy future without natural gas.

Democratic leaders have an opportunity to help our party rebrand energy priorities coming out of the 2024 elections. Keeping the lights on and bills affordable should never be partisan goals. A balanced energy approach — natural gas working with renewables — is the key to achieving both.

Mary Landrieu served as Democratic senator from Louisiana from 1997 to 2015. Terry McAuliffe served as Democratic governor of Virginia from 2014 to 2018. Both serve on the Leadership Council of Natural Allies for a Clean Energy Future.

data.title: King Charles on former Prince Andrew's arrest: 'The law must take its course'
data.description:
King Charles III weighed in Thursday after his brother, former Prince Andrew, was reportedly arrested, saying, “the law must take its course.”

“I have learned with the deepest concern the news about Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor and suspicion of misconduct in public office,” Charles said in a statement obtained by The Hill’s sister network NewsNation. “What now follows is the full, fair and proper process by which this issue is investigated in the appropriate manner and by the appropriate authorities.”

“In this, as I have said before, they have our full and wholehearted support and co-operation. Let me state clearly: the law must take its course,” he continued. “As this process continues, it would not be right for me to comment further on this matter.”

Moundbatten-Windsor was reportedly taken into custody Thursday on “suspicion of misconduct” while in public office. The reported arrest came as new revelations surrounding his connection with the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein were uncovered.

According to the BBC, Mountbatten-Windsor was detained at the Sandringham Estate in Norfolk, England.

Within the last year, the scandal surrounding Epstein has heated up. The Trump administration faced pressure from both sides of the aisle to release files related to the probe of the disgraced financier after a joint memo from the Justice Department (DOJ) and FBI seemingly closed the book on the Epstein case.

Under the Epstein Files Transparency Act, a bipartisan law signed by President Trump late last year, the DOJ released millions of documents to the public, with heavy redactions officials said were done to protect victims. Lawmakers also had the chance to view unredacted files earlier this month.

Recent files revealed that Mountbatten-Windsor gave confidential information to Epstein during his time serving as the U.K.’s Special Trade Representative for Trade and Investment, per the BBC.

Since his ties to Epstein came into the spotlight, Andrew was stripped of his royal titles and moved out of his royal residence.

data.title: Moore says he prays for Trump, feels 'bad for him'
data.description:
Maryland Gov. Wes Moore (D) on Wednesday said that he feels bad for President Trump and prays for him, even as the two are embroiled in a public feud over the National Governors Association meetings and a sewage spill in the Potomac.

“I have no desire to have beef with the president of the United States,” Moore told The Associated Press. “I didn’t run for governor like, man, I can’t wait so me and the president can go toe to toe. I have no desire on that. 

“But the fact that he is waking up in the middle of the night and tweeting about me, I just, I pray for him and I just feel bad for him because that has just got to be a really, really hard existence,” added Moore, who is frequently mentioned in Democratic signals as a potential presidential candidate.

Trump has singled out Moore and Colorado Gov. Jared Polis (D) for criticism, and did not invite the two to a black-tie dinner with governors and their families at the White House this Saturday. In response, Moore told CBS News earlier this month he does not plan on attending a Friday White House meeting between the president and governors — an annual gathering. 

Earlier this week, the president also blamed Moore, Virginia Gov. Abigail Spanberger (D) and Washington, D.C., Mayor Muriel Bowser (D) for the sewage spill in the Potomac River. A collapsed section of the Potomac Interceptor in Montgomery County, Md., resulted in 243 million gallons of untreated wastewater seeping into the river within five days last month. 

The interceptor is owned and operated by local utility DC Water, while the collapsed section sits on land overseen by the National Park Service. DC Water erected a temporary bypass pipe on Jan. 24 to halt the flow of untreated water into the river, but local and state public health officials are encouraging residents not to come into contact with the Potomac due to elevated concentrations of E. coli bacteria.

The president wrote on Truth Social Tuesday that Moore, Spanberger and Bowser “must act, IMMEDIATELY” and said the federal government “can fix” the issue.

“ACT FAST. I am awaiting your call,” he added.

Bowser declared a local emergency Wednesday and asked for the federal government to reimburse 100 percent of the costs incurred by the district government and DC Water.

While the Maryland Environment and Health departments are assisting with the response to the collapse, Moore said Wednesday, “How this has anything to do with Maryland, I have no idea.”

“I think [Trump] just woke up and just said, I hate Maryland so I’m just going to introduce them into a conversation,” the governor told the AP. “This literally has nothing to do with us, with the exception of the fact that when we first heard about what happened, that I ordered our team to assist Washington, D.C.”

data.title: Is Trump going too far with immigration enforcement?
data.description:
A February poll indicates that 65 percent of people in the U.S. think the Trump administration’s immigration enforcement measures go “too far.” Are they right? Or is the administration just faithfully executing the law as required by the “take care” provision in the U.S. Constitution?

Certainly, the current administration is doing a better job than the previous one of executing the provisions in the Immigration and Nationality Act.

Joe Biden’s enforcement policy was based on the assumption that the majority of deportable aliens in the U.S. are “contributing members of our communities.” According to its Guidelines for the Enforcement of Civil Immigration Law, “The fact an individual is a removable noncitizen therefore should not alone be the basis of an enforcement action against them.”

Enforcement measures under Biden were restricted to deportable aliens “who pose a threat to national security, public safety, and border security and thus threaten America’ s well-being.” In other words, the previous administration restricted deportations to the illegal immigrants they thought should be deported.

The current administration is being criticized for going in the other direction, pursuing all deportable immigrants — noncriminal as well as criminal. That executes the mandate that Congress created in federal law with its classes of deportable aliens list, which specifies the grounds under which “any alien … be removed.”  

Border czar Tom Homan is faithfully executing that mandate. He has said that it doesn’t matter whether deportable aliens are violent criminals or are just here to make a better life for themselves. “The law is the law,” Homan said last year. “If you’re in the country illegally, you’re not off the table.” 

When President Trump began his second term in office, he quickly reinstated a regulation from his first term that had been rescinded under Biden, expanding the application of expedited proceedings to the maximum extent allowed by law. Under such proceedings, immigrants in the country illegally are removed summarily unless they establish a credible fear of persecution or torture. If this fear is established, they are entitled to a hearing before an immigration judge.

Trump’s expansion of expedited removal applies to illegal immigrants who have “not been admitted or paroled” into the U.S. and have not been physically present here for two or more years. Previous administrations did not implement this part of the expedited removal provisions.

During Trump’s first term, the expansion was challenged in court, unsuccessfully. The U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit held that “federal law commits to the ‘sole and unreviewable discretion’ of the Secretary of Homeland Security” decisions about “expedited removal.”

But the biggest obstacle to faithfully executing the law has been resistance in sanctuary cities from city officials and from angry protesters who have interfered with field operations. The administration had to deploy approximately 3,000 armed immigration officers to conduct enforcement operations in Minnesota.

Was this going too far? At a congressional hearing on Feb. 10, Acting ICE Director Todd Lyons explained why it was necessary. He testified that ICE is “facing the deadliest operating environment in our agency’s history.”

“In Fiscal Year 2025, death threats against ICE personnel increased by more than 8,000 percent,” said Lyons. “Assaults on our officers have skyrocketed by over 1,400 percent. One officer in Minnesota had his finger bitten off by a left-wing protestor, egged on by elected officials characterizing our officers as the ‘gestapo’ or ‘secret police.’ The families of ICE personnel have been made to feel unsafe in their own homes.”

Lyons has firsthand knowledge of threats to families because “his own family has been targeted.”

If local authorities in Minnesota had permitted ICE to pick up criminal aliens when they are released from jail or prison, DHS would not have had to surge enforcement officers into local communities to find them. This would have prevented confrontations between ICE officers and protesters, and it would have reduced the number of noncriminal aliens who were arrested, because the ICE officers wouldn’t have come into contact with so many of them.

In West Virginia, in comparison, local authorities held criminal aliens for ICE to pick up when they were released from custody. This made it possible for ICE to conduct a two-week, statewide enforcement operation that arrested around 650 illegal aliens with little disruption. 

In any case, the surge of enforcement officers into communities in Minnesota is ending because an agreement has been reached with Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison (D) to permit state and local jails to hold criminal aliens for ICE when they are released.

The demonstrations and the lack of cooperation from local authorities did not stop ICE officers from enforcing immigration law in Minnesota. It just resulted in violent confrontations and tragic deaths that did not benefit anyone. 

Nolan Rappaport was detailed to the House Judiciary Committee as an Executive Branch Immigration Law Expert for three years. He subsequently served as an immigration counsel for the Subcommittee on Immigration, Border Security and Claims for four years. Prior to working on the Judiciary Committee, he wrote decisions for the Board of Immigration Appeals for 20 years.

data.title: Moltbook's 'vibe-coded' breach is the future of security failures
data.description:
Earlier this month, the now viral social network Moltbook exposed 1.5 million API authentication tokens and 35,000 email addresses within days of launch. The cause: a single misconfigured database setting.

The founder didn’t write a single line of code. Instead, he “vibe-coded” the entire platform, prompting an AI assistant to build it. The result attracted widespread buzz — and Elon Musk’s praise — but it also left millions of credentials in an unlocked database, accessible to anyone who looked.

Security researchers found the exposed Supabase API key within minutes. The platform was missing Row Level Security, a basic configuration that acts as the first line of defense. It’s the digital equivalent of leaving your front door unlocked. But a single unlocked door can become a master key to cloud infrastructure, AI services, payment processors and customer databases. 

Moltbook is not an outlier but a preview of what happens when AI-powered development outpaces security understanding.

This isn’t the first time development speed has outpaced security. Early web applications leaked databases through SQL injection. Mobile apps shipped with hardcoded API keys. Cloud storage buckets exposed terabytes of customer data. Each wave brought preventable vulnerabilities, eventually fixed through painful lessons and better tooling. 

But AI-assisted development accelerates this pattern. AI coding assistants and IDE agents have become ubiquitous, integrated into every major development environment from VS Code to JetBrains. Millions of developers now rely on them daily. Tools that once took weeks to misconfigure now take mere minutes — and without fundamental configuration knowledge, developers can cause significant damage before their first commit. Interconnected systems mean each misconfiguration carries much greater risk. 

Configuration errors are the oldest vulnerability in the security handbook, a leading cause of incidents and the entry point for most ransomware attacks. What’s changed is velocity and abstraction. 

When developers spent weeks writing backend code, they understood every dependency and made conscious security choices. When AI generates that backend in minutes, those critical decisions become invisible, abstracted into prompts that prioritize making it work over making it secure.  

Even when organizations detect misconfigurations, the gap between detection and remediation is fatal. Remediation takes 63 to 104 days on average. Attackers exploit the same weaknesses in hours.

Fixing a misconfiguration safely requires mapping every dependency: What services rely on this setting? Which authentication flows will break? What workflows depend on current access patterns? Change one database permission without understanding these connections, and you might close a security hole while opening a business continuity disaster. Security teams that can identify problems but lack the contextual awareness to fix them safely often choose inaction over risk.

Today’s microservices architecture, API-first design, and OAuth-connected ecosystems mean every application is a potential pivot point. An attacker who compromises one service can often reach dozens more. The “blast radius” of configuration failures has expanded exponentially. 

Machine-generated applications worsen the problem as each service comes with dependencies its creator may not fully understand. AI agents themselves, running on millions of endpoints, create additional risk. Unlike standard processes, these agents operate with elevated permissions and access to sensitive systems, yet they’re often misconfigured by default. AI generates functional code but can’t map the invisible threads connecting that code to other systems. When a misconfiguration is discovered, determining how to fix it without triggering cascading failures becomes exponentially harder. 

There’s a dangerous skills gap widening. Millions know how to use AI coding tools, but what we need are people who understand both AI-powered development and security fundamentals. That intersection is nearly empty. 

Most security professionals don’t use AI development tools, so they can’t anticipate the vulnerabilities these tools create. Most AI-native developers lack security training, so they don’t  recognize dangerously misconfigured code. 

Even rarer are those who can safely remediate configuration issues in complex systems. This requires architectural understanding and the ability to trace dependencies, predict side effects, and implement fixes that close vulnerabilities without breaking functionality. It’s expertise that takes years to develop.  

The people who bridge these worlds are becoming the industry’s scarcest resource. This raises an obvious question: Won’t AI eventually generate secure code by default? Perhaps it will. But security isn’t just about code quality as much as it’s about context.  

AI can’t know your organization’s threat model, risk tolerance or regulatory requirements. It can’t decide whether a particular API should be public or private. Even if AI perfectly generates secure code, it can’t understand the operational dependencies that make remediation safe. These are judgment calls requiring understanding of how your specific business operates. 

Moltbook’s founder fixed the exposed database within hours. But this isn’t the last Moltbook we’ll see. The pattern is clear: We’re building software faster than we can secure it. Organizations need capabilities that map dependencies, validate changes and provide rollback mechanisms when fixes create unexpected problems. Without intelligent remediation, the detection-to-fix gap will continue widening. 

In an AI-powered world where trust is the scarcest resource, organizations that can remediate as fast as they detect will have an insurmountable competitive advantage. The rest will be explaining their breaches. 

Tal Kollender, a former hacker, is CEO of cybersecurity startup Remedio.

data.title: Watch live: Trump hosts inaugural Board of Peace meeting in DC
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday morning will host the first meeting of his Board of Peace in Washington.

The recently formed organization has been tasked with supervising postwar restoration efforts in Gaza amid a fragile ceasefire between Israel and Hamas — including the disarmament of the Palestinian militant group as part of the second phase of the Trump’s 20-point peace plan.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio, envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, U.S. Ambassador to the U.N. Mike Waltz, Bulgarian High Rep. Nickolay Mladenov and former British prime minister Tony Blair, among others, will also give remarks at the meeting.

Representatives from over 40 countries who have signed on to the board are also expected to attend the event.

“Since the President and his world-class team ended the war between Israel and Hamas last October, we have maintained the ceasefire, delivered historic amounts of humanitarian aid, and freed every single living and dead hostage,” White House Deputy Press Secretary Anna Kelly said in a statement. “The Board of Peace will continue this historic success and prove itself to be the most consequential international body in history.”

The event is scheduled to begin at 9 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.
